title: Exercise 1
statement: You are given an accumulation function \( a(t) = 1 + 0.1t \). An amount of \( 1000 \) is invested at time \( 0 \). Determine the amount of interest earned during year \( 8 \).
solution_steps: The interest earned during year \( 8 \) is \( I_8 = A(8) - A(7) = 1800 - 1700 = 100 \).
generalization: For year \( n \), the interest earned is \( I_n = A(n) - A(n-1) = 1000(1 + 0.1n) - 1000(1 + 0.1(n-1)) = 100 \).
answer_text: The amount of interest earned during year \( 8 \) is \( 100 \).

title: Exercise 2
statement: You are given an accumulation function \( a(t) = bt^2 + c \). A deposit of \( 100 \) at time \( 0 \) is worth \( 200 \) at time \( 10 \). Find the accumulated value at time \( 15 \) of \( 500 \) invested at time \( 0 \).
solution_steps: Since \( A(t) = 100\,a(t) \) for the initial deposit of \(100\), the condition at time \(10\) gives \( 100a(10) = 200 \), so \( a(10) = 2 \).
Also, by definition of an accumulation function, \( a(0) = 1 \).
From \( a(t) = bt^2 + c \), we get \( a(0) = c = 1 \).
The accumulated value at time \(15\) of \(500\) invested at time \(0\) is \( 500a(15) = 500(3.25) = 1625 \).
generalization: Once the accumulation function \( a(t) \) is identified, the accumulated value at time \( t \) of an amount \( P \) invested at time \( 0 \) is \( P\,a(t) \).
answer_text: The accumulated value at time \( 15 \) is \( \boxed{1625} \).

title: Exercise 3
statement: An investment of \( 1000 \) is made into a fund at time \( t = 0 \). The fund develops the following balances over the next four years: \[ \begin{array}{c|ccccc} t & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline A(t) & 1000 & 1060 & 1113 & 1146 & 1181 \end{array} \] Find \( i_1 \) and \( i_3 \), the effective rates of interest for years one and three respectively.
solution_steps: The effective rate of interest for year \( n \) is given by \( i_n = \dfrac{A(n)-A(n-1)}{A(n-1)} \).
generalization: If the accumulated values \( A(0), A(1), \dots \) are known, then the effective rate for year \( n \) is \( i_n = \dfrac{A(n)-A(n-1)}{A(n-1)} \).
answer_text: The effective rates are \( i_1 = 6\% \) and \( i_3 \approx 2.96\% \).

title: Exercise 4
statement:
Gertrude deposits \( 10{,}000 \) in a bank. During the first year, the bank credits an annual effective rate of interest \( i \). During the second year, the bank credits an annual effective rate of interest \( i - 5\% \). At the end of two years, she has \( 12{,}093.75 \) in the bank.

What would Gertrude have in the bank at the end of three years, if the annual effective rate of interest were \( i + 9\% \) for each of the three years?
solution_steps: Set up the two-year accumulation equation: \( 10000(1+i)(1+i-0.05)=12093.75 \).
Solve the quadratic equation. The valid solution is \( i = 0.125 = 12.5\% \).
The new annual effective rate for each of the three years is \( i+9\% = 12.5\%+9\% = 21.5\% \).
Therefore, the amount in the bank at the end of three years is \( \boxed{17{,}942.41} \).
generalization: When annual effective rates vary by year, accumulate by multiplying one annual factor for each year. If rates are linked algebraically, first solve for the unknown rate, then apply the required accumulation factors.
answer_text: The amount in the bank at the end of three years is \( \boxed{17{,}942.41} \).

title: Exercise 5
statement:
A deposit of \( X \) is made into a fund which pays an annual effective interest rate of \( 6\% \) for \( 10 \) years.

At the same time, \( X/2 \) is deposited into another fund which pays an annual effective rate of discount \( d \) for \( 10 \) years.

The amounts of interest earned over the \( 10 \) years are equal for both funds.

Calculate \( d \).
solution_steps: The accumulated value of the first fund after 10 years is \( X(1.06)^{10} \), so the interest earned is \( X\big((1.06)^{10}-1\big) \).
For an annual effective discount rate \( d \), the annual accumulation factor is \( \dfrac{1}{1-d} \).

Thus the accumulated value of the second fund after 10 years is \( \dfrac{X}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{1-d}\right)^{10} \), so the interest earned is \( \dfrac{X}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{1-d}\right)^{10}-1\right] \).
generalization: For an annual effective discount rate \( d \), the annual accumulation factor is \( \dfrac{1}{1-d} \). When comparing interest earned on two funds, write each interest amount as accumulated value minus principal, then solve the resulting equation.
answer_text: The annual effective discount rate is \( \boxed{9.05\%} \).

title: Exercise 6
statement:
[SOA 16; SOA-IT.020] David can receive one of the following two payment streams:

i. \( 100 \) at time \( 0 \), \( 200 \) at time \( n \) years, and \( 300 \) at time \( 2n \) years

ii. \( 600 \) at time \( 10 \) years

At an annual effective interest rate of \( i \), the present values of the two streams are equal.

Given \( v^n = 0.76 \), calculate \( i \).
generalization: When \( v^n \) is known, higher powers such as \( v^{2n} \) can be obtained directly. Then reduce the present value equation to a single equation in \( v^m \), and finally solve for \( i \) using \( v = \dfrac{1}{1+i} \).
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate is \( \boxed{3.50\%} \).

title: Exercise 7
statement: Sam invested \( \$500 \) in a bank. After two years, the fund increased to \( \$561.80 \). What was the annual effective interest rate offered by the bank?
solution_steps: The initial amount is \( A(0)=500 \), and the accumulated amount after two years is \( A(2)=561.80 \).
Using the constant effective interest rate formula, \( A(t)=A(0)(1+i)^t \).
generalization: When an amount \( A(0) \) accumulates to \( A(t) \) over \( t \) years at a constant annual effective interest rate \( i \), use \( A(t)=A(0)(1+i)^t \), so \( i = \left(\dfrac{A(t)}{A(0)}\right)^{1/t}-1 \).
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate was \( \boxed{6\%} \).

title: Exercise 8
statement: Beth invests \( \$300 \) in a fund earning a \( 1\% \) monthly effective interest rate. How much is in her fund at the end of eight months?
solution_steps: Since the interest rate is given as a monthly effective interest rate, define \( t \) in terms of months.
The initial investment is \( A(0)=300 \), the monthly effective interest rate is \( i=0.01 \), and the time is \( t=8 \).
generalization: When the effective interest rate is given per period, measure \( t \) in that same period. For a monthly effective interest rate, \( t \) should be measured in months.
answer_text: Beth has \( \boxed{\$324.86} \) at the end of eight months.

title: Exercise 9
statement: James invests \( \$100 \) in a bank. The fund earns an annual effective interest rate of \( 10\% \). Calculate the fund accumulation at the end of six months.
solution_steps: The interest rate is an annual effective interest rate, so define \( t \) in terms of years.
Six months is half a year, so \( t=0.5 \).
The initial investment is \( A(0)=100 \), and the annual effective interest rate is \( i=0.10 \).
generalization: When an annual effective interest rate is used for a fraction of a year, the exponent is the fraction of the year. For six months, use \( t=\frac{1}{2} \).
answer_text: The fund accumulation is \( \boxed{\$104.88} \) at the end of six months.

title: Exercise 10
statement: Toby wants to have \( \$500 \) in one year. How much does he need to invest today if the account credits a \( 7\% \) annual effective interest rate?
solution_steps: We are not given the initial investment. Instead, we are given the amount at the end of one year.
The desired accumulated value is \( A(1)=500 \), and the annual effective interest rate is \( i=0.07 \).
generalization: To find the amount that must be invested today to reach a future value, discount the future value back to the present. With annual effective interest rate \( i \), the present value of \( A(t) \) due in \( t \) years is \( A(0)=\dfrac{A(t)}{(1+i)^t} \).
answer_text: Toby needs to invest \( \boxed{\$467.29} \) today.

title: Exercise 11
statement: Fund A is invested at an annual effective interest rate of \(3\%\). Fund B is invested at an annual effective interest rate of \(2.5\%\). At the end of \(20\) years, the total in the two funds is \(10{,}000\). At the end of \(31\) years, the amount in Fund A is twice the amount in Fund B. Calculate the total in the two funds at the end of \(10\) years.
solution_steps: Let \(A\) and \(B\) be the amounts initially invested in Fund A and Fund B, respectively.
At the end of \(20\) years, the total in the two funds is \(10{,}000\), so \(A(1.03)^{20}+B(1.025)^{20}=10{,}000\).
At the end of \(31\) years, the amount in Fund A is twice the amount in Fund B, so \(A(1.03)^{31}=2B(1.025)^{31}\).
Substitute this expression for \(A\) into the equation at time \(20\): \(\dfrac{2B(1.025)^{31}}{(1.03)^{31}}(1.03)^{20}+B(1.025)^{20}=10{,}000\).
The total of the two funds at the end of \(10\) years is \(A(1.03)^{10}+B(1.025)^{10}\).
generalization: When two funds accumulate at different effective interest rates, write equations at the time points given in the problem. Solve for the initial deposits, then accumulate or discount to the required time.
answer_text: The total in the two funds at the end of \(10\) years is \(\boxed{7{,}569.07}\).

title: Exercise 12
statement: Bob invests \(X\) in an account that pays interest at an annual effective rate of \(8\%\). Ten years later, he withdraws \(X\). Ten years after that, he withdraws \(1{,}000\), which exhausts the account. Calculate \(X\).
solution_steps: Accumulate all cash flows to time \(20\).
The initial deposit \(X\) accumulates for \(20\) years, so its accumulated value at time \(20\) is \(X(1.08)^{20}\).
The withdrawal of \(X\) at time \(10\) accumulates for \(10\) years to time \(20\), so its accumulated value at time \(20\) is \(X(1.08)^{10}\).
Since the final withdrawal of \(1{,}000\) exhausts the account at time \(20\), we have \(X(1.08)^{20}-X(1.08)^{10}=1{,}000\).
generalization: When several cash flows occur at different times, choose one comparison date and move every cash flow to that date using the appropriate accumulation or discount factor. Then set the value of inflows equal to the value of outflows.
answer_text: Bob invested \(\boxed{399.68}\).

title: Example 1.1.6
statement: Sam invests \(200\) in a bank. The fund earns an annual effective rate of discount of \(9.0909\%\). What will the fund value be at the end of five years?
solution_steps: Since \((1-d)^{-1}\) is the one-period accumulation factor associated with an effective discount rate, the accumulated value after five years is \(A(5)=200(1-0.090909)^{-5}\).
generalization: When an effective discount rate \(d\) is used to accumulate a value forward in time, use the accumulation factor \((1-d)^{-1}\) for each period. Over \(t\) periods, the accumulated value is \(A(t)=A(0)(1-d)^{-t}\).
answer_text: The fund accumulates to \(\boxed{322.10}\) at the end of five years.

title: Example 1.1.7
statement: Fund A offers a monthly effective discount rate of \(1\%\). Jack deposited \(350\) into Fund A four years ago. Now, he withdraws all his funds. How much does he withdraw?
solution_steps: Since there are \(12\) months in a year, four years correspond to \(48\) months.
Since \((1-d)^{-1}\) is the one-period accumulation factor associated with an effective discount rate, the accumulated value after \(48\) months is \(A(48)=350(1-0.01)^{-48}\).
generalization: When the discount rate is effective per month, the time period must be measured in months. If money is invested for \(n\) months at a monthly effective discount rate \(d\), the accumulated value is \(A(n)=A(0)(1-d)^{-n}\).
answer_text: Jack withdraws \(\boxed{566.99}\) from the fund.

title: Example 1.1.7
statement: Given an \(8\%\) annual effective interest rate, calculate the equivalent annual effective discount rate, \(d\), and the discount factor, \(v\).
solution_steps: The effective interest rate is \(8\%\). Thus, \(i=0.08\).
Recall that the effective discount rate is \(d=\dfrac{i}{1+i}\).
Recall that the discount factor is \(v=\dfrac{1}{1+i}\).
generalization: Given an effective interest rate \(i\), the equivalent effective discount rate is \(d=\dfrac{i}{1+i}\), and the discount factor is \(v=\dfrac{1}{1+i}\).
answer_text: The equivalent annual effective discount rate is \(\boxed{7.407\%}\), and the discount factor is \(\boxed{0.9259}\).

title: Exercise 16
statement: A deposit of \(X\) is made into a fund which pays an annual effective interest rate of \(6\%\) for \(10\) years. At the same time, \(\dfrac{X}{2}\) is deposited into another fund which pays an annual effective rate of discount of \(d\) for \(10\) years. The amounts of interest earned over the \(10\) years are equal for both funds. Calculate \(d\).
solution_steps: The amount of interest earned is the difference between the initial amount and the amount at the end of \(10\) years.
For the first fund, the accumulated value after \(10\) years is \(X(1.06)^{10}\). Therefore, the interest earned is \(X(1.06)^{10}-X\).
For the second fund, the annual effective rate of discount is \(d\), so the accumulation factor over \(10\) years is \((1-d)^{-10}\).
The accumulated value of the second fund after \(10\) years is \(\dfrac{X}{2}(1-d)^{-10}\). Therefore, the interest earned is \(\dfrac{X}{2}(1-d)^{-10}-\dfrac{X}{2}\).
Since the amounts of interest earned are equal, \(X(1.06)^{10}-X=\dfrac{X}{2}(1-d)^{-10}-\dfrac{X}{2}\).
generalization: When comparing interest earned by two funds, compute interest as accumulated value minus initial deposit for each fund. If one fund uses an effective discount rate \(d\), remember that its accumulation factor over \(n\) periods is \((1-d)^{-n}\).
answer_text: The annual effective rate of discount is \(\boxed{9.049\%}\).

title: Exercise 17
solution_steps: The effective rate of interest for year \(3\) is \(i_3=\dfrac{A(3)-A(2)}{A(2)}\).
The effective rate of interest for year \(7\) is \(i_7=\dfrac{A(7)-A(6)}{A(6)}\).
generalization: An amount function of the form \(A(t)=C(1+i)^t\) represents compound interest with a constant effective interest rate \(i\) each period. This is the interest assumption used in almost every FM problem.
answer_text: The effective rates are \(\boxed{i_3=i_7=8\%}\).

title: Exercise 18
statement: An investment of \(1{,}000\) is made into a fund at time \(t=0\). The fund develops the following balances over the next \(4\) years: \[ \begin{array}{c|ccccc} t & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline A(t) & 1{,}000 & 1{,}060 & 1{,}113 & 1{,}146 & 1{,}181 \end{array} \] If \(5{,}000\) is invested in the same fund at time \(1\), find the amount of interest earned on this deposit between time \(2\) and time \(3\).
solution_steps: Since the new deposit is made into the same fund at time \(1\), its value at later times grows in proportion to the original fund values after time \(1\).
Let \(A^*(t)\) be the accumulated value at time \(t\) of the \(5{,}000\) deposited at time \(1\).
The interest earned between time \(2\) and time \(3\) is \(I_3^*=A^*(3)-A^*(2)\).
generalization: If a deposit is made into a fund at time \(k\), and the fund has amount function values \(A(t)\), then the accumulated value at time \(t\ge k\) of a deposit \(P\) made at time \(k\) is \(P\dfrac{A(t)}{A(k)}\).
answer_text: The amount of interest earned between time \(2\) and time \(3\) is \(\boxed{155.66}\).

title: Exercise 19
statement: You are given the accumulation function \(a(t)=2t^2+3t+1\). Determine \(i_9\), the effective interest rate during the ninth year.
solution_steps: The ninth year is the period from time \(8\) to time \(9\).
The effective interest rate during year \(9\) is \(i_9=\dfrac{a(9)-a(8)}{a(8)}\).
generalization: For an accumulation function \(a(t)\), the effective interest rate during year \(n\) is \(i_n=\dfrac{a(n)-a(n-1)}{a(n-1)}\).
answer_text: The effective interest rate during the ninth year is \(\boxed{24.18\%}\).

title: Exercise 20
statement: Gertrude deposits \(10{,}000\) in a bank. During the first year, the bank credits an annual effective rate of interest \(i\). During the second year, the bank credits an annual effective rate of interest \(i-5\%\). At the end of two years, she has \(12{,}093.75\) in the bank. What would Gertrude have in the bank at the end of three years, if the annual effective rate of interest were \(i+9\%\) for each of the three years?
solution_steps: Set up the two-year accumulation equation: \(10{,}000(1+i)(1+i-0.05)=12{,}093.75\).
The new annual effective rate is \(i+9\%=0.125+0.09=0.215\).
generalization: When annual effective rates vary by year, multiply the corresponding annual accumulation factors. If the rates depend on an unknown parameter, first use the given accumulated value to solve for that parameter, then apply the required accumulation formula.
answer_text: Gertrude would have \(\boxed{17{,}936.13}\) in the bank at the end of three years.

title: Exercise 21
statement: [SOA 22; SOA-IT.027] Bruce and Robbie each open up new bank accounts at time \(0\). Bruce deposits \(100\) into his bank account, and Robbie deposits \(50\) into his. Each account earns the same annual effective interest rate. The amount of interest earned in Bruce's account during the \(11\)th year is equal to \(X\). The amount of interest earned in Robbie's account during the \(17\)th year is also equal to \(X\). Calculate \(X\).
solution_steps: The amount of interest earned in any account is the difference between its value at the end of a period and its value at the beginning of the period.
The amount of interest earned in Bruce's account during the \(11\)th year is \(100(1+i)^{11}-100(1+i)^{10}=X\).
The amount of interest earned in Robbie's account during the \(17\)th year is \(50(1+i)^{17}-50(1+i)^{16}=X\).
Since the interest rate is positive, divide by \(50(1+i)^{10}i\) to get \(2=(1+i)^6\).
generalization: The interest earned during year \(n\) is the accumulated value at time \(n\) minus the accumulated value at time \(n-1\). For a deposit \(P\) at annual effective interest rate \(i\), this interest is \(P(1+i)^n-P(1+i)^{n-1}=P(1+i)^{n-1}i\).
answer_text: The amount of interest is \(\boxed{38.88}\).

title: Exercise 22
statement: John can earn the following rates for the next five years: Year \(1\): effective rate of interest \(=4\%\); Year \(2\): effective rate of discount \(=5\%\); Year \(3\): effective rate of interest \(=6\%\); Years \(4\) and \(5\): effective rate of discount \(=10\%\). John needs to have \(1{,}000\) in \(5\) years. How much should he invest now?
solution_steps: We need the present value at time \(0\) of \(1{,}000\) paid at time \(5\).
To move backward through a year with an effective interest rate \(i\), divide by \(1+i\).
To move backward through a year with an effective discount rate \(d\), multiply by \(1-d\).
Starting from time \(5\), the year \(5\) discount rate gives the factor \(1-0.10=0.90\).
The year \(4\) discount rate also gives the factor \(1-0.10=0.90\).
The year \(3\) interest rate gives the factor \((1.06)^{-1}\).
The year \(2\) discount rate gives the factor \(1-0.05=0.95\).
The year \(1\) interest rate gives the factor \((1.04)^{-1}\).
generalization: When moving backward in time, use \((1+i)^{-1}\) for an effective interest rate and \(1-d\) for an effective discount rate. Apply the factors year by year in reverse chronological order.
answer_text: John should invest \(\boxed{698.02}\) now.

title: Exercise 23
solution_steps: The quantity \(v_5\) is the discount factor for year \(5\). It moves money backward from time \(5\) to time \(4\).
generalization: For an accumulation function \(a(t)\), the discount factor for year \(n\) is \(v_n=\dfrac{a(n-1)}{a(n)}\). If \(a(t)=(1+i)^t\), then \(v_n=\dfrac{1}{1+i}\) for every year \(n\).
answer_text: The discount factor for year \(5\) is \(\boxed{0.92593}\).

title: Exercise 24
statement: [SOA 16; SOA-IT.020] David can receive one of the following two payment streams: i. \(100\) at time \(0\), \(200\) at time \(n\) years, and \(300\) at time \(2n\) years; ii. \(600\) at time \(10\) years. At an annual effective interest rate of \(i\), the present values of the two streams are equal. Given \(v^n=0.76\), calculate \(i\).
solution_steps: The present value of the first payment stream is \(100+200v^n+300v^{2n}\).
The present value of the second payment stream is \(600v^{10}\).
Since the present values are equal, \(600v^{10}=100+200v^n+300v^{2n}\).
generalization: When two payment streams have equal present values, discount each payment to time \(0\), equate the present values, and solve for the unknown interest rate. If \(v^n\) is given, use powers such as \(v^{2n}=(v^n)^2\) to simplify the equation.
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate is \(\boxed{3.5\%}\).

title: Exercise 25
statement: The amount of interest paid on a loan of \(X\) for one year is \(108\). The equivalent amount of discount paid on a loan of \(X\) for one year is \(100\). Determine \(X\).
solution_steps: The amount of interest paid is \(Xi=108\).
The amount of discount paid is \(Xd=100\).
Recall the relationship between the effective rate of interest and the effective rate of discount: \(d=\dfrac{i}{1+i}\).
generalization: For a one-period loan of amount \(X\), the amount of interest paid is \(Xi\), while the amount of discount paid is \(Xd\). The effective rate of interest and the effective rate of discount satisfy \(d=\dfrac{i}{1+i}\) and \(i=\dfrac{d}{1-d}\).
answer_text: The loan amount is \(\boxed{1{,}350}\), so the correct choice is E.

title: Exercise 26
statement: At a certain rate of compound interest, \(1\) grows to \(3\) in \(x\) years, \(3\) grows to \(14\) in \(y\) years, and \(1\) grows to \(21\) in \(z\) years. Determine what \(5\) grows to in \(z-x-y\) years.
solution_steps: Write each given fact as an accumulation factor equation using \((1+i)^t\).
Since \(1\) grows to \(3\) in \(x\) years, we have \((1+i)^x=3\).
Since \(3\) grows to \(14\) in \(y\) years, we have \(3(1+i)^y=14\), so \((1+i)^y=\dfrac{14}{3}\).
Since \(1\) grows to \(21\) in \(z\) years, we have \((1+i)^z=21\).
We want the accumulated value of \(5\) after \(z-x-y\) years, which is \(5(1+i)^{z-x-y}\).
generalization: Under compound interest, time exponents can be added and subtracted using the laws of exponents. If \((1+i)^a\), \((1+i)^b\), and \((1+i)^c\) are known, then \((1+i)^{c-a-b}=\dfrac{(1+i)^c}{(1+i)^a(1+i)^b}\).
answer_text: \(5\) grows to \(\boxed{\dfrac{15}{2}}\).

title: Exercise 27
statement: A loan is made for five years at a simple interest rate of \(12\%\) per annum. What is the equivalent annual effective rate of discount during the fourth year of the loan?
solution_steps: Let the original loan amount be \(1\).
Since the loan uses simple interest at \(12\%\) per annum, the accumulation function is \(a(t)=1+0.12t\).
The fourth year is the period from time \(3\) to time \(4\).
Compute the loan balance at time \(3\): \(a(3)=1+0.12(3)=1.36\).
Compute the loan balance at time \(4\): \(a(4)=1+0.12(4)=1.48\).
The effective rate of discount during the fourth year is \(d_4=\dfrac{a(4)-a(3)}{a(4)}\).
generalization: Under simple interest with rate \(i\), the accumulation function is \(a(t)=1+it\). The effective rate of discount during year \(n\) is \(d_n=\dfrac{a(n)-a(n-1)}{a(n)}\).
answer_text: The equivalent annual effective rate of discount during the fourth year is \(\boxed{8.108\%}\).

title: Exercise 28
statement: At a rate of simple interest \(i\), \(10\) will accumulate to \(15\) after \(x\) years. What will \(20\) accumulate to at a simple interest rate of \(2i\) after \(5x\) years?
solution_steps: Under simple interest, the accumulated value is \(A(t)=P(1+it)\).
We are given that \(10\) accumulates to \(15\) after \(x\) years at simple interest rate \(i\), so \(10(1+ix)=15\).
Now compute the accumulated value of \(20\) at simple interest rate \(2i\) after \(5x\) years.
generalization: Under simple interest, the accumulated value depends on the product \(it\). If a condition gives the value of \(ix\), it can be substituted directly into other simple interest expressions involving multiples of \(i\) and \(x\).
answer_text: \(20\) accumulates to \(\boxed{120}\).

title: Exercise 29
statement: An investment earns \(10\%\) compound interest for each complete year and \(8\%\) simple interest for each partial year. A \(\$100\) investment is made on January \(1\), \(2008\). What is the accumulated value of the investment on July \(1\), \(2012\)?
solution_steps: From January \(1\), \(2008\), to January \(1\), \(2012\), there are \(4\) complete years.
From January \(1\), \(2012\), to July \(1\), \(2012\), there is \(0.5\) of a year.
For the \(4\) complete years, use compound interest at \(10\%\): \(100(1.10)^4\).
For the partial year, use simple interest at \(8\%\), giving the factor \(1+0.08(0.5)\).
generalization: When complete years use compound interest and partial years use simple interest, first accumulate through the full years using \((1+i)^n\), then multiply by the simple interest factor \(1+jt\) for the partial year.
answer_text: The accumulated value is \(\boxed{\$152.27}\).
